title: The Digital Memory We Cannot Afford to Lose: Knowledge Rights 21 Signs the Our Future Memory Statement
content:
Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) has signed the Our Future Memory Statement on the Four Digital Rights of Memory Institutions. We join the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions (IFLA), the International Council on Archives (ICA), the Wikimedia Foundation, and hundreds of libraries, archives, and museums across six continents in calling on policymakers to ensure that memory institutions retain online the same rights they have always held offline: the right to collect, to preserve, to provide access, and to cooperate across borders.

The timing is not coincidental. The urgency is real.

In May 2026, Mark Graham, the director of the Wayback Machine, described it as “collateral damage caught up in the conflict between AI companies and publishers.” News publishers, including some of the world’s most prominent titles, have begun blocking the Internet Archive from archiving their websites. The stated reason is concerns about AI scraping. The effect is that the public record of those publications, part of our collective memory, is no longer being preserved.
snippet: Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) has signed the Our Future Memory Statement on the Four Digital Rights of Memory Institutions. We join the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions
creator: KR21
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Sofia University Library Awarded “Library of the Year 2025” in Bulgaria for Leadership in Open Science
content:
Blog post by Ana Lazarova

On May 14, 2026, the Sofia University Library “St. Kliment Ohridski” – the academic library of Bulgaria’s oldest and largest university – received Bulgaria’s Library of the Year 2025 award. The distinction was presented at the XVI Annual Library Awards ceremony, organised by the Bulgarian Library and Information Association during the XX National Library Week. The University library was nominated by the Academic Council of Sofia University in recognition of its achievements in open science and digitisation.

The main reason for the nomination and the award was the library’s active implementation of the secondary publication mechanisms adopted at the national level in recent years.

In late 2023, Bulgaria adopted a Secondary Publishing Right (SPR) into its copyright legislation – Article 60, paragraphs 2 and following of the Copyright and Neighbouring Rights Act. This created an imperative regime preventing publishers from overriding specific author reuse rights.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: National Workshop on Public Lending Right: Strengthening Moldova’s Path Toward EU Integration
content:
Blog post by Mariana Harjevschi

On 23 and 24  April 2026, coinciding with World Book and Copyright Day, the National Workshop on Public Lending Right (PLR) took place in Chișinău in a hybrid format. The event brought together policymakers, international experts, and representatives of libraries, authors’ organisations, and cultural institutions to advance discussions on introducing Public Lending Right in the Republic of Moldova.

The workshop was organised by the State Agency on Intellectual Property (AGEPI) with the support of the EU4IP project and Swiss Partnership Moldova. It formed part of the broader national effort to align copyright legislation with the EU acquis and to explore sustainable policy options for implementing PLR.

The opening session featured contributions from institutional and international partners, highlighting the importance of coordinated action between government bodies, libraries, and the cultural sector. Discussions underscored that the implementation of PLR requires not only legal adjustments but also institutional readiness and sustainable financing mechanisms.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: 10 Takeaways From the Twin EU Consultations About the Landscape for Copyright Reform
content:
In the past weeks, we have seen the launch of two consultations relating to the European Union’s legislation about copyright, and how it affects the ability of researchers, educators, learners and the libraries that support them to work.

The results of these will, a priori, play an important role in shaping the direction of travel of the European Union’s future work in this area, and in particular, potential legislation next year (tentatively titled ‘Directive enhancing the copyright environment for European creativity and innovation’).

The first consultation is an open Call for Evidence (25 June deadline) on what is needed in order to create ‘a better European copyright environment for European creativity and innovation’, allowing for open feedback but with a number of themes highlighted as being of particular interest.
